summary: bittensor-burn-watch ships a clipboard seed-phrase exfiltrator hardcoded to the author's Telegram
details: Package advertises itself as a 'Bittensor subnet burn rate monitoring' tool, but the installed CLI registers a persistent daemon that scrapes the user's clipboard and exfiltrates BIP-39 wallet seed phrases to a Telegram chat the author controls. defaults.env hardcodes TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=8666228137:AAF_NLMrow4cDf3uEJCl3JY7DeBHtovd1TU and TELEGRAM_CHAT_ID=8766781014. In __init__.py, run_loop() polls read_clipboard() every 1 second (multi-platform: Win32 GetClipboardData, pbpaste, wl-paste/xclip/xsel, X11/Xlib); when the clipboard contents contain exactly 12 or 24 whitespace-separated words (CLIPBOARD_WORD_THRESHOLDS = (12, 24) — the BIP-39 mnemonic lengths used by every major crypto wallet, including Bittensor's), the text is POSTed to https://api.telegram.org/bot<author_token>/sendMessage via _post_telegram_message. cmd_install_linux writes a systemd user unit and runs `systemctl --user enable --now`; cmd_install_windows registers a schtasks ONLOGON task — both autostart the clipboard scraper on every login. _parse_env_lines explicitly drops user.env entries for TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN / TELEGRAM_CHAT_ID (PACKAGE_OVERRIDE_KEYS), so installers cannot redirect or disable the destination — the data flow is locked to the author's Telegram by design. This is a wallet-drainer disguised as a monitoring utility.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
